theorem_statement:
Colorful Carathéodory Theorem.

Let \(X_1,\dots,X_{d+1}\) be finite subsets of \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Assume that for each \(i\), the origin belongs to the convex hull of \(X_i\):
\[
0 \in \operatorname{conv}(X_i).
\]
Then there exist points \(x_i \in X_i\) for \(i=1,\dots,d+1\) such that
\[
0 \in \operatorname{conv}\{x_1,\dots,x_{d+1}\}.
\]
In other words, one can choose one point from each color class so that the origin lies in the convex hull of the resulting colorful set.
raw_proof_text:
We prove the theorem by a distance-minimization argument.

Because each \(X_i\) is finite, there are only finitely many colorful sets
\[
S=\{x_1,\dots,x_{d+1}\}, \qquad x_i\in X_i.
\]
For each such colorful set \(S\), its convex hull \(\operatorname{conv}(S)\) is compact, so there exists at least one point of \(\operatorname{conv}(S)\) of minimal Euclidean distance from the origin.

Among all colorful sets \(S\), choose one for which the distance
\[
\operatorname{dist}(0,\operatorname{conv}(S))
\]
is minimal. Fix such a minimizing colorful set
\[
S^*=\{x_1,\dots,x_{d+1}\}, \qquad x_i\in X_i,
\]
and let \(z\in \operatorname{conv}(S^*)\) be a point of minimal norm in \(\operatorname{conv}(S^*)\). If \(z=0\), then \(0\in \operatorname{conv}(S^*)\), and we are done. So suppose for contradiction that
\[
z\neq 0.
\]

Since \(z\in \operatorname{conv}(S^*)\), there exist coefficients \(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_{d+1}\ge 0\) with
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{d+1} \lambda_i = 1
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
z = \sum_{i=1}^{d+1} \lambda_i x_i.
\]
Among all such convex representations of \(z\) using points of \(S^*\), choose one with the smallest possible number of nonzero coefficients. Let
\[
I := \{ i : \lambda_i > 0 \}.
\]
Then \(z\in \operatorname{conv}\{x_i : i\in I\}\), and by minimality of the support, the points \(\{x_i : i\in I\}\) are affinely independent. In particular,
\[
|I| \le d+1.
\]
If in fact \(|I|=d+1\), then the \(d+1\) points \(x_1,\dots,x_{d+1}\) are affinely independent and form a simplex containing \(z\) in its relative interior. We will show this is impossible.

Because \(z\) is the point of minimal norm in the convex set \(\operatorname{conv}(S^*)\), the supporting-hyperplane condition holds at \(z\): for every \(y\in \operatorname{conv}(S^*)\),
\[
(y-z)\cdot z \ge 0.
\]
Indeed, for any such \(y\), the segment
\[
(1-t)z + ty \in \operatorname{conv}(S^*) \qquad (0\le t\le 1)
\]
lies in the convex hull, and since \(z\) minimizes the squared norm on this segment, the derivative at \(t=0\) must be nonnegative:
\[
\frac{d}{dt}\Big|_{t=0} \|(1-t)z+ty\|^2 = 2(y-z)\cdot z \ge 0.
\]
Applying this to each vertex \(x_i\in S^*\), we obtain
\[
(x_i-z)\cdot z \ge 0,
\qquad\text{hence}\qquad
x_i\cdot z \ge \|z\|^2
\qquad (i=1,\dots,d+1).
\]
Now use the convex representation of \(z\):
\[
\|z\|^2 = z\cdot z = \sum_{i=1}^{d+1} \lambda_i (x_i\cdot z).
\]
Since each term \(x_i\cdot z\) is at least \(\|z\|^2\), and the coefficients \(\lambda_i\) are nonnegative and sum to 1, the weighted average can equal \(\|z\|^2\) only if equality holds for every index with \(\lambda_i>0\). Therefore
\[
x_i\cdot z = \|z\|^2
\qquad\text{for every } i\in I.
\]
Thus all active vertices \(x_i\) lie in the affine hyperplane
\[
H := \{y\in\mathbb{R}^d : y\cdot z = \|z\|^2\}.
\]
But then the points \(\{x_i : i\in I\}\) lie in a proper affine hyperplane of \(\mathbb{R}^d\), so they are affinely dependent. This contradicts the minimal-support choice of the representation of \(z\). Hence \(|I|\le d\), so at least one index \(j\in\{1,\dots,d+1\}\) satisfies
\[
\lambda_j = 0.
\]
It follows that
\[
z \in \operatorname{conv}(S^*\setminus\{x_j\}).
\]

Now use the assumption that
\[
0\in \operatorname{conv}(X_j).
\]
So there exist coefficients \(\alpha_p\ge 0\) for \(p\in X_j\), summing to 1, such that
\[
0 = \sum_{p\in X_j} \alpha_p p.
\]
Take the dot product with \(z\):
\[
0 = \sum_{p\in X_j} \alpha_p (p\cdot z).
\]
Since the \(\alpha_p\) are nonnegative and sum to 1, not every \(p\cdot z\) can be positive. Therefore there exists some point
\[
x_j'\in X_j
\]
with
\[
x_j'\cdot z \le 0.
\]

Now form the new colorful set
\[
S' := (S^*\setminus\{x_j\}) \cup \{x_j'\}.
\]
Because \(z\in \operatorname{conv}(S^*\setminus\{x_j\})\), every point on the segment joining \(z\) to \(x_j'\) belongs to \(\operatorname{conv}(S')\). In particular, for
\[
0\le t\le 1,
\qquad
w_t := (1-t)z + t x_j' \,\in\, \operatorname{conv}(S').
\]
Consider the squared norm
\[
\phi(t) := \|w_t\|^2 = \|(1-t)z + t x_j'\|^2.
\]
Differentiate at \(t=0\):
\[
\phi'(0) = 2(x_j'-z)\cdot z = 2(x_j'\cdot z - \|z\|^2).
\]
Since \(x_j'\cdot z \le 0\) and \(z\neq 0\), we have
\[
\phi'(0) \le -2\|z\|^2 < 0.
\]
Therefore for all sufficiently small \(t>0\),
\[
\|w_t\| < \|z\|.
\]
But \(w_t\in \operatorname{conv}(S')\), so this means
\[
\operatorname{dist}(0,\operatorname{conv}(S')) < \operatorname{dist}(0,\operatorname{conv}(S^*)).
\]
This contradicts the choice of \(S^*\) as a colorful set minimizing the distance from the origin.

The contradiction arose from assuming \(z\neq 0\). Hence necessarily
\[
z=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
0 \in \operatorname{conv}(S^*).
\]
Since \(S^*\) is colorful, this proves that one can choose one point from each \(X_i\) so that the origin lies in the convex hull of the chosen points.

This completes the proof.
